condition.qualitative_description: Surface water body total area is declining at more than 5 ha/year and fewer than 3 distinct water bodies exist in the MWS, indicating shrinking storage infrastructure.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: A declining trend in total SWB area combined with a low count of water bodies signals that existing surface storage infrastructure is contracting — through siltation, structural failure, or encroachment — rather than expanding. In semi-arid hard rock plateau terrain, surface tanks and check dams are the primary irrigation buffer between monsoon and rabi seasons; their loss directly translates to insufficient water for post-kharif cropping.

condition.qualitative_description: The mean ratio of SWB rabi area to kharif area is below 0.35, indicating that water bodies dry out rapidly after the monsoon and cannot sustain rabi irrigation.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: A low rabi-to-kharif SWB area ratio reveals that surface water bodies lose most of their storage quickly after the monsoon ends, leaving little water available for rabi and zaid seasons. This pattern is consistent with siltation reducing effective storage capacity — a well-documented problem in hard rock terrain tanks where silt accumulation reduces infiltration and holding volume over time. Dashora et al. (2019) demonstrated that a 5-year-old check dam in Gujarat had filled completely with silt at 40 cm/year, eliminating its recharge and storage function entirely.

condition.qualitative_description: Fewer than 5 cumulative soil and water conservation or irrigation works recorded under MGNREGA for the MWS, indicating historically low investment in water harvesting infrastructure.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Low MGNREGA SWC investment means that opportunities to rehabilitate tanks, construct check dams, or build percolation ponds have not been taken up. In semi-arid Deccan plateau contexts, MGNREGA-funded tank desilting and check dam construction are the primary public mechanisms for restoring irrigation water availability. Dashora et al. (2019) showed that manual desilting of a check dam increased recharge by 84% in the following year, with a benefit-cost ratio of 4.1 across four structures — underscoring the high returns from maintenance investment that low NREGA counts suggest is absent.

condition.qualitative_description: No named river passes through or adjacent to the MWS, and the nearest agricultural cooperative is more than 15 km away, indicating both absence of surface diversion potential and limited institutional access to credit for irrigation investment.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: The absence of a perennial or seasonal river eliminates the possibility of diversion or lift irrigation from a flowing source, making the MWS entirely dependent on tank and groundwater storage. Combined with poor cooperative access — which limits farmers' ability to obtain institutional credit for pump sets, drip systems, or tank repair — this composite signal indicates structural isolation from both natural and financial irrigation resources. In interior Karnataka and Andhra Pradesh, cooperative proximity is a key determinant of whether smallholders can invest in micro-irrigation or solar pumps.

condition.qualitative_description: Farmers report that wells or borewells have become deeper over recent years, or that pumping costs have increased substantially, suggesting declining water tables that raise the cost of groundwater-based irrigation beyond the reach of marginal farmers.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: In hard rock aquifers of the Deccan plateau, groundwater is stored in shallow weathered and fractured zones typically 10–30 m deep. As water tables decline due to over-extraction or reduced recharge from degraded surface storage, well depths increase and lifting costs rise disproportionately. This disadvantages marginal and small farmers who cannot afford deeper borewells or higher energy costs, effectively excluding them from groundwater-based irrigation even when aquifer water remains accessible to wealthier farmers with deeper infrastructure.

overall_reasoning_note: Irrigation infrastructure failure in semi-arid hard rock plateau contexts is confirmed when at least two of the primary signals align: declining SWB area trend (sig_01), rapid post-monsoon drying of water bodies (sig_02), and low MGNREGA investment history (sig_03). The composite signal (sig_04) and qualitative well depth signal (sig_05) amplify the diagnosis but do not confirm it independently. The most actionable entry point is sig_03 — low NREGA SWC count combined with declining SWB area strongly justifies tank desilting and check dam rehabilitation as priority interventions, given documented benefit-cost ratios above 4:1 in comparable hard rock settings. Always verify whether the SWB area decline is driven by siltation (reversible through desilting) versus structural failure or encroachment (requiring different interventions) before recommending solutions.

confounders.confounder: Meteorological drought causing apparent irrigation infrastructure failure through reduced inflows rather than structural degradation
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Compare mean_annual_precipitation_mm against long-term baseline and check drought_severe_return_period. If SWB area decline co-occurs with below-average rainfall years, drought is the primary driver and infrastructure is functioning normally but undersupplied. In this case, the rabi-to-kharif SWB ratio would be low even in years with intact infrastructure. Cross-check trend_swb_total_area_ha — if the decline is persistent across both wet and dry years, siltation or structural failure is more likely than drought alone.

confounders.confounder: Groundwater stress (over-extraction) rather than surface irrigation infrastructure failure as the primary cause of water scarcity
confounders.how_to_distinguish: If swb_count and swb_total_area_ha are stable or increasing but farmers still report water scarcity, the pathway is more likely groundwater depletion than surface infrastructure failure. Check whether nrega_swc_count is high (indicating investment has occurred) but water scarcity persists — this points to groundwater stress as the dominant pathway. The irrigation_challenges pathway is confirmed when surface storage metrics are declining and investment is low, not when surface storage is adequate but groundwater is depleted.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Have the wells or borewells in your village become deeper over the past 5–10 years? Roughly how deep are they now compared to before — for example, have they gone from 20 feet to 50 feet, or from 50 feet to 100 feet? Have farmers noticed that pumping costs have increased significantly?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: If well depths have increased substantially (more than 10 metres over 5–8 years) and pumping costs have risen, this confirms that groundwater-based irrigation is becoming economically inaccessible for marginal farmers, amplifying the irrigation challenges diagnosis and supporting solar pump installation as a priority intervention to reduce energy costs. If well depths are stable, the water scarcity is more likely attributable to surface storage failure alone.

choices.label: Well depth has stayed roughly the same (within ~1 m)
Wells have deepened by more than 2–3 m in the last 5–10 years
Springs or shallow wells have dried up permanently or for much longer

sources.title: Hydrologic and cost benefit analysis at local scale of streambed recharge structures in Rajasthan (India) and their value for securing irrigation water supplies
sources.relevance_note: Documents check dam recharge dynamics, siltation impacts on storage and infiltration capacity, desilting effects (manual desilting increased recharge by 84%), and benefit-cost ratios (average 4.1) for streambed recharge structures in hard rock terrain in India — directly supporting tank desilting recommendations and SWB area decline as a diagnostic signal.

sources.title: Why is the Arkavathy River drying? A multiple-hypothesis approach in a data-scarce region
sources.relevance_note: Demonstrates that groundwater pumping and channel fragmentation — not climate change — are primary drivers of surface water decline in a southern Indian hard rock catchment, supporting the diagnostic logic that declining SWB area in semi-arid Deccan contexts reflects anthropogenic infrastructure and extraction pressures rather than rainfall change alone.

sources.title: Climate Change 2014 - Synthesis Report
sources.relevance_note: Provides global context for increasing water scarcity under climate variability, relevant to the confounder distinguishing meteorological drought from infrastructure failure as causes of seasonal water unavailability in semi-arid India.
